title: Dot-com bubble bursts
description: The NASDAQ crashes after years of speculative investment in internet companies. Hundreds of startups shut down, and tech employment begins a multi-year decline.

title: Post-9/11 economic shock
description: The September 11 attacks trigger a sharp economic downturn. Tech spending freezes as businesses cut budgets, accelerating layoffs that were already underway from the dot-com bust.

title: Telecom industry collapse
description: WorldCom files for bankruptcy amid accounting fraud. The telecom sector, a major employer within the Information industry, sheds hundreds of thousands of jobs.

title: Sarbanes-Oxley Act signed
description: New corporate governance regulations increase compliance costs for tech companies. The law reshapes how public companies operate in response to Enron and WorldCom scandals.

title: Tech hiring recovery begins
description: After three years of decline, tech companies start hiring again. Google's IPO in August 2004 signals renewed investor confidence in the sector.

title: Web 2.0 hiring boom
description: Social media and user-generated content platforms drive a new wave of tech hiring. Companies like Facebook, YouTube, and Twitter are scaling rapidly.

title: iPhone launches, mobile era begins
description: Apple's iPhone creates an entirely new platform for software development. The mobile app economy will eventually employ hundreds of thousands of developers.

title: Great Recession begins
description: The U.S. enters its worst recession since the 1930s. While tech is hit less hard than construction and finance, hiring freezes spread across the industry.

title: Lehman Brothers collapses
description: The investment bank's bankruptcy triggers a global financial crisis. Tech companies cut budgets and freeze hiring as the economy enters freefall.

title: ARRA stimulus signed
description: The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act includes $7.2 billion for broadband expansion and tech infrastructure, helping stabilize the sector.

title: Tech hiring recovery post-recession
description: Tech companies lead the economic recovery. Cloud computing and mobile development create new job categories, and Silicon Valley hiring accelerates.

title: Cloud computing hiring surge
description: AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud drive massive demand for cloud engineers, DevOps, and infrastructure roles. Enterprise IT spending shifts from hardware to cloud services.

title: Facebook IPO
description: Facebook goes public at a $104 billion valuation, the largest tech IPO in history at the time. Social media companies accelerate hiring across engineering and content moderation.

title: Big data and analytics boom
description: Data scientist becomes the 'sexiest job of the 21st century.' Companies across industries hire data teams, driving demand for analytics, ML, and data engineering talent.

title: Enterprise cloud adoption accelerates
description: Large enterprises move workloads to the cloud at scale. AWS revenue doubles, and demand for cloud architects, SREs, and platform engineers skyrockets.

title: Gig economy platforms scale up
description: Uber, Lyft, and DoorDash expand aggressively, hiring thousands of engineers while reshaping debates about worker classification and labor law.

title: H-1B visa restrictions tighten
description: Executive orders increase scrutiny on H-1B visas, affecting tech companies' ability to hire foreign talent. Denial rates increase sharply over the next two years.

title: Pre-pandemic tech hiring peak
description: Tech employment reaches record levels as FAANG companies, enterprise software firms, and startups compete aggressively for talent. Salaries surge.

title: US-China trade war escalates
description: Tariffs and export controls create uncertainty for hardware companies and chipmakers. Huawei is banned from US technology, disrupting global supply chains.

title: COVID-19 lockdowns begin
description: The pandemic triggers the fastest labor market collapse in U.S. history. Tech is hit less than hospitality and retail, but startups freeze hiring and some cut staff.

title: CARES Act and PPP loans
description: Massive fiscal stimulus helps companies retain workers. The $2 trillion package includes forgivable loans that prevent many tech companies from doing layoffs.

title: Remote work boom
description: Tech companies embrace permanent remote work, expanding hiring beyond traditional tech hubs. Zoom, Slack, and collaboration tools see explosive growth.

title: Pandemic tech hiring surge
description: E-commerce, cloud, and digital transformation drive unprecedented tech hiring. Companies like Amazon, Shopify, and Zoom add thousands of employees.

title: SPAC and crypto hiring boom
description: Speculative investment in SPACs and cryptocurrency drives hiring at fintech and crypto companies. Coinbase goes public; NFT platforms scale engineering teams.

title: The Great Resignation
description: Record numbers of workers quit their jobs seeking better pay, remote work, and work-life balance. Tech quits rates hit historic highs as workers have unprecedented leverage.

title: Infrastructure Investment Act
description: The $1.2 trillion infrastructure bill includes $65 billion for broadband expansion, creating demand for telecom and network engineering roles.

title: Fed begins rate hike cycle
description: The Federal Reserve starts raising interest rates to combat inflation. Higher rates squeeze startup funding, slow growth spending, and trigger a reassessment of tech valuations.

title: Crypto market crashes
description: Terra/Luna and Three Arrows Capital collapse wipe out billions. Crypto exchanges and Web3 companies begin mass layoffs, with Coinbase cutting 18% of staff.

title: CHIPS and Science Act signed
description: The $280 billion law subsidizes domestic semiconductor manufacturing. Intel, TSMC, and Samsung announce major U.S. fab construction, creating future manufacturing and engineering jobs.

title: Meta lays off 11,000 employees
description: Meta cuts 13% of its workforce, the first mass layoff in the company's history. This kicks off a wave of tech layoffs across the industry that will continue into 2023.

title: ChatGPT launches
description: OpenAI releases ChatGPT, triggering an AI arms race. Every major tech company pivots resources toward generative AI, reshaping hiring priorities across the industry.

title: Google, Amazon, Microsoft mass layoffs
description: The tech layoff wave peaks as Google cuts 12,000, Amazon cuts 18,000, and Microsoft cuts 10,000. Over 150,000 tech workers are laid off in the first quarter alone.

title: Silicon Valley Bank collapses
description: SVB's failure sends shockwaves through the startup ecosystem. Many companies scramble to access payroll funds, briefly threatening a startup employment crisis.

title: AI hiring boom begins
description: Demand for AI/ML engineers, prompt engineers, and AI safety researchers explodes. Companies race to build or integrate large language models, creating a new talent war.

title: WGA and SAG-AFTRA strikes
description: Writers and actors strike over AI usage and streaming residuals. The entertainment industry shutdown affects thousands of tech-adjacent jobs in streaming and content platforms.

title: Tech layoffs continue into 2024
description: Despite a recovering stock market, layoffs continue at Google, Amazon, Discord, Twitch, and others. Companies shift from growth-at-all-costs to efficiency and profitability.

title: AI infrastructure buildout accelerates
description: Nvidia's market cap surpasses $3 trillion as hyperscalers invest billions in GPU clusters and data centers. Demand for AI infrastructure, MLOps, and data engineering talent surges.

title: Fed begins cutting rates
description: The Federal Reserve starts lowering interest rates, easing financial conditions for tech companies and startups. Venture capital activity begins to recover.

title: New tariffs shake tech supply chains
description: Broad new tariffs on imports from China and other countries create uncertainty for hardware companies and raise costs for data center buildouts.

title: AI agent adoption wave
description: Companies deploy AI agents for coding, customer service, and data analysis at scale. The shift creates new roles in AI oversight while automating some traditional software development tasks.

title: DeepSeek disrupts AI market
description: Chinese AI lab DeepSeek releases competitive open-source models at a fraction of the cost, challenging the dominance of U.S. AI companies and reshaping the competitive landscape.

title: GPT-3 released by OpenAI
description: OpenAI releases GPT-3 with 175 billion parameters, demonstrating dramatic capabilities in text generation, translation, and code writing. Sparks widespread discussion about AI replacing knowledge workers.

title: DALL-E announced
description: OpenAI reveals DALL-E, an AI system that creates images from text descriptions. Signals the beginning of generative AI's expansion beyond text into creative fields.

title: GitHub Copilot launched
description: GitHub and OpenAI launch Copilot, an AI pair programmer that suggests code in real-time. First mainstream AI tool directly targeting software developer workflows.

title: Stable Diffusion released
description: Stability AI releases Stable Diffusion as open source, making powerful image generation accessible to anyone. Triggers a wave of AI art tools and concerns in creative industries.

title: ChatGPT launches
description: OpenAI releases ChatGPT, reaching 100 million users in two months. Fundamentally shifts public perception of AI capabilities and triggers an industry-wide race to build AI products.

title: GPT-4 released
description: OpenAI launches GPT-4 with multimodal capabilities. Passes the bar exam and medical licensing exams. Companies across every sector begin integrating GPT-4 into products.

title: Claude 2 and Llama 2 released
description: Anthropic launches Claude 2 while Meta open-sources Llama 2. The market shifts from OpenAI monopoly to multi-provider competition, expanding AI hiring demand.

title: Google launches Gemini
description: Google releases Gemini, its most capable AI model, directly competing with GPT-4. Signals Google's full commitment to the AI race and triggers increased AI hiring across Big Tech.

title: Claude 3 model family
description: Anthropic releases Claude 3 Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku — a family of models at different capability and price points. Sets new benchmarks in reasoning and analysis.

title: GPT-4o released
description: OpenAI releases GPT-4o with native multimodal capabilities including real-time voice. Makes advanced AI capabilities more accessible through the free tier.

title: Claude 3.5 Sonnet released
description: Anthropic releases Claude 3.5 Sonnet, matching or exceeding GPT-4o on most benchmarks at lower cost. Becomes the preferred model for many coding and analysis workflows.

title: Llama 3.1 405B released
description: Meta releases Llama 3.1 with a 405-billion parameter open model competitive with proprietary alternatives. Dramatically expands the open-source AI ecosystem.

title: OpenAI o1 reasoning model
description: OpenAI releases o1, a model that 'thinks before it answers' using chain-of-thought reasoning. Represents a new paradigm in AI architecture focused on deliberate reasoning.

title: DeepSeek R1 disrupts the market
description: Chinese AI lab DeepSeek releases R1, matching frontier model performance at a fraction of the cost. Sends shockwaves through the AI industry and causes a significant Nvidia stock drop.

title: Claude 4 / Opus released
description: Anthropic releases Claude 4, setting new standards for agentic AI capabilities. Models can now reliably execute multi-step workflows, accelerating AI agent adoption in enterprises.
